title: From Homeschooling Mom to Executive Coach: Building Leaders at Home and Work
guestBio: Amanda Jaggard is an Executive Coach at Novus Global and co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute. After earning her Master's in Leadership and Ministry and homeschooling her sons for nearly a decade, she transitioned into executive coaching in 2014. As a single mom with one son at UCLA and another graduating high school, she applies executive coaching principles to parenting and leadership development.
excerpt: Executive coach and single mom Amanda Jaggard shares how she transformed from homeschooling mother to successful business leader while raising her sons on commitments instead of rules. Her approach to building culture at home offers powerful lessons for workplace leadership.
metaDescription: Discover how executive coach Amanda Jaggard applies leadership principles to parenting, building culture through commitments instead of rules.
keyTakeaways: Commitments create stronger accountability than rules because they come from internal motivation rather than external enforcement.
Building culture at home requires intentional language, clear agreements, and mutual respect between parents and children.
Executive coaching principles can be effectively applied to parenting to develop leadership qualities in children.
Transitioning career focus during different seasons of motherhood requires strategic planning and gradual implementation.
The Meta Performance Institute model shows that coaches need both coaching skills and business acumen to succeed.
Preparing for empty nest season while building a thriving career requires balancing present family needs with future goals.
Creating a household culture built on honor and respect prepares children to be leaders in their own lives.

pullQuotes.text: We raised our boys on commitments instead of rules, fostering a household culture built on respect and honor.

timestamps.label: Amanda's transition from homeschooling to coaching
Living values at work and home

content:
What happens when you take the principles that transform executives and apply them to raising the next generation? Amanda Jaggard has spent years answering that question, and her approach will challenge everything you think you know about parenting, leadership, and building culture.

As an Executive Coach at Novus Global and co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute, Amanda brings a unique perspective to leadership development. But her journey to this role wasn't traditional. After earning her Master's in Leadership and Ministry, she dedicated nearly a decade to homeschooling her sons before fully stepping into executive coaching in 2014. This experience gave her something most business coaches lack: real-world practice in developing leaders from the ground up.

The Power of Commitments Over Rules

One of Amanda's most revolutionary insights centers on the difference between rules and commitments in both parenting and leadership. While rules come from external authority and often breed resentment or rebellion, commitments emerge from internal motivation and personal ownership. This distinction has shaped how she raised her sons and how she approaches executive coaching.

In her household, Amanda didn't rely on a long list of rules that needed constant enforcement. Instead, she worked with her sons to establish commitments they could own and honor. This approach required more upfront investment in conversation and relationship-building, but it created a foundation of mutual respect that continues to serve them as young adults.

The same principle applies in business leadership. When you help team members make commitments rather than simply follow rules, you tap into their intrinsic motivation and create sustainable behavior change. It's the difference between compliance and engagement, between following orders and taking ownership.

Building Culture Intentionally

Culture doesn't happen by accident, whether in your home or your organization. Amanda's approach to creating culture involves three key elements: intentional language, clear agreements, and consistent respect. These aren't just nice ideals—they're practical tools that shape daily interactions and long-term outcomes.

The language you use in your home sets the tone for everything else. Amanda was deliberate about speaking to her sons with the same respect she would show adults, while maintaining appropriate boundaries and expectations. This modeled the kind of communication they would need to succeed in their own relationships and careers.

Clear agreements replaced arbitrary rules in her household. Instead of "because I said so," conversations centered on "what did we agree was important?" This approach taught her sons to think critically about their choices and understand the reasoning behind family expectations.

Consistent respect means honoring each person's dignity while maintaining healthy hierarchies. As a parent, Amanda held authority without becoming authoritarian. She could make final decisions while still valuing input and treating her children as individuals worthy of consideration.

The Meta Performance Institute Model

Amanda's work co-founding the Meta Performance Institute addresses a critical gap in the coaching industry. Many talented coaches struggle not because they lack coaching skills, but because they haven't developed the business acumen needed to build sustainable practices. The Institute trains coaches in both the art of coaching and the practical skills required to thrive professionally.

This dual focus reflects Amanda's broader philosophy: excellence requires both heart and strategy, both relationship and systems, both vision and execution. Whether you're raising children or developing leaders, you need both the soft skills of connection and the hard skills of structure.

Navigating Career Transitions as a Mother

Amanda's journey from homeschooling mother to successful executive coach illustrates how women can strategically navigate career transitions during different seasons of motherhood. Her approach wasn't to abandon her family responsibilities, but to gradually build her professional presence while maintaining her commitment to her sons.

This required careful planning and patience. She began developing her coaching skills while still homeschooling, slowly building experience and credibility. By the time her sons were older and more independent, she was positioned to step more fully into her professional role.

The key was treating her career development as a long-term strategy rather than an all-or-nothing decision. She invested in relationships, continued learning, and looked for opportunities to contribute professionally even when her primary focus was on family.

Preparing for the Next Season

Now, with one son at UCLA and another about to graduate high school, Amanda faces the transition many mothers dread: the empty nest season. But her approach to this transition reflects the same intentionality she's brought to other areas of life.

Rather than viewing this as loss, she sees it as an opportunity to apply the leadership principles she's developed to a new season of life and career. The culture she built in her home prepared her sons to thrive independently, and the skills she developed as a mother continue to serve her as an executive coach.

This perspective offers hope and practical guidance for other mothers facing similar transitions. Your investment in your children's development doesn't end when they leave home—it evolves. The leadership skills you've developed as a mother can become powerful tools for professional impact.

Amanda Jaggard's story proves that the principles of great leadership are universal. Whether you're developing executives or raising children, building organizational culture or creating a thriving home environment, the fundamentals remain the same: respect, commitment, clear communication, and intentional culture-building.

The question isn't whether you have what it takes to be a leader—you're already leading. The question is whether you're ready to be as intentional about that leadership as Amanda has been about hers.
